data.title: Jobs Report Shows Economy Is Slowing; Recession Warning Signs Abound.
data.description:
NEW YORK (January 19) - The Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) today reported 50,000 December jobs, according to the Establishment Survey, a collection of job creation data from businesses replying to the survey. The number was well below the consensus estimate of 66,000. The private payroll number was just 37,000, after taking account of 13,000 government jobs added. October and November revisions cost another net 76,000 jobs lost.

For the three months from October, average monthly jobs creation was more than 22,000 net jobs lost.

The BLS’s Household Survey, which polls the number of people taking jobs, and is viewed as eliminating workers holding more than one job, printed well above the Establishment Survey at 183,000 new jobs..

The unemployment rate and the U6 rate was 4.4% and 8.4%, respectively. The “U6” is the percentage of the population that is unemployed, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force, plus total employed part time for economic reasons, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force. Some 46,000 people left the labor force, pushing the Labor Force Participation Rate down slightly, to 62.4%.

Let’s look at our exclusive schedule of November and December Jobs Creation by Average Weekly Wages:

As you can see, Jobs were lost in virtually ever sector, save for Leisure and Hospitality and what we call "government adjacent" jobs in Education and Health Services (which includes the Healthcare & Social Assistance sector) which tend to be funded by governmental programs. Leisure & Hospitality is the lowest wage sector we cover.

We do not include “official” government jobs payrolls in our chart, but government gained 13,000 jobs.in in December.

As is our practice at the end of a quarter, we show here the average weekly real wage increase achieved during the quarter.

Economy Generally

It’s clear the Fed has botched rates for several meetings and that private, business-oriented hiring is slowing. Absent job creation in the Private education and health services sectors, which includes Health care and social assistance, and which sectors have little correlation to interest rates, average jobs creation over the last three months has been over 69,000 jobs lost.

If the Phillips Curve is to be believed, the poor jobs creation over the last several months may have played a role in the disinflation we have seen this year. It should also not be lost on the Fed that the labor force participation rate is likely to increase in 2026 due to work requirements for SNAP recipients. (Work requirements for Medicaid start in 2027.)

While the additional workers entering the workforce might be absorbed from the generous fiscal policy of the “Big Beautiful Bill,” high interest rates will inhibit the ability of small and medium sized businesses - the companies most likely to hire workers with a poor work history -- to access the cap that will allow them to make use of first year depreciation and lower hiring costs (from tax free tips), etc. The Fed discount rates should probably be at 2.25 to 2.75%.

PROGNOSTICATIONS

The data point to a slowing economy. However, we think there may be substantially increased capex owing to the Big Beautiful Bill. But that won’t be reflected, likely, until 2026Q2 or Q3. In view of today’s release, we are reducing our estimate of 2025 Q4 to 1.5%, +/- 25 bps. For 2026, we expect much the same for 2026Q1. We expect the Fed will stand pat with interest rates for the time being, unless Chair Powell resigns his position before President Trump appoints a new Fed chair, which has been rumored.

Other Data Points

These are data points we use to assess the overall standing of the economy and are provided here for information only.

GDP printed at 4.3% in 2025Q3, according to the Preliminary Estimate released December 23rd. Core GDP, or real final sales to domestic purchasers, which eliminates some of the “noise” in the GDP report, like inventories and net exports, printed at 3.0%. We expect that number will be revised downward substantially in later estimates.

The personal consumption expenditures (PCE) price index increased 2.8 percent, compared with an increase of 2.1 percent. Excluding food and energy prices, the PCE price index increased 2.9 percent, compared with an increase of 2.6 percent. Core PCE inflation is, reportedly, the Fed’s preferred measure of inflation.

The Institute for Supply Management's Manufacturer's Purchasing Managers Index (PMI) printed at 47.9 for December. The overall manufacturing sector has been declining for 10 months.

The November services Index grew to 54.4 from November’s 52.6.

The Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) for November 2025 printed Wednesday with 303,000 fewer job openings than in October.

Privately-owned housing units authorized by building permits in October were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1,412,000.This is 0.2 percent below the revised September rate of 1,415,000 and is 1.1 percent below the October 2024 rate of 1,428,000.

Privately-owned housing starts in October were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1,246,000. This is 4.6 percent (±11.2 percent)* below the revised September estimate of 1,306,000 and is 7.8 percent (±11.7 percent)* below the October 2024 rate of 1,352,000.

Personal income increased $94.5 billion (0.4 percent at a monthly rate) in September according to revised estimates released by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis on December 23rd.. Disposable personal income (DPI)—personal income less personal current taxes—increased $75.9 billion (0.3 percent) and personal consumption expenditures (PCE) increased $65.1 billion (0.3 percent).

RealClearMarkets/TIPP Economic Optimism Index for December printed at 47.9%. The January number will not print until the 13th.

______________________________

Note: Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political/geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe to be underperforming, and include strategies that we would recommend for the companies of our clients. Others discuss new management strategies we believe will fail. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions here regarding whether to buy, sell, or hold such companies, however, assume the company will not change its current practices.)

data.title: Amazon: The World's No. 1 Hyperscaler Is Definitely Not 'AI Hype'
data.description:
You'd have to be living under an ice-sheet in Antarctica not to have heard the constant hand-wringing within the broad investment analyst community about the threat of the "AI-bubble" and "AI-hype." Yet as I have shown in many of my Seeking Alpha articles, the leading technology companies like Nvidia (NVDA), Alphabet (GOOG), and Broadcom (AVGO) are generating very strong free-cash-flow and can easily afford the cap-ex required to take advantage of growth opportunities moving forward - see "Broadcom: What So Many Analysts And Investors Got Wrong (Buy The Dip)" and "Google: The AI-Leader OpenAI Is Still Chasing (But Will Never Catch)."

While Amazon (AMZN) may not currently have as strong a FCF profile as compared to Broadcom and Google, and the stock has clearly lagged both those (see graphic below) over the past three years, in my opinion AMZN still belongs in the same tier-1 class of AI leaders. Amazon is a Buy and one of my favorite technology stocks for 2026 based on its relative lack of media exposure compared to the leading AI companies, the company's often over-looked strengths of its "economic flywheel," and an attractive valuation. Today, I'll dip into all those areas.

Investment Thesis

As you know, Amazon's AWS was arguably the world's first hyperscaler and according to Statista it remains #1 by quite a wide margin over #2 Microsoft (MSFT) Azure as of Q3 2025:

While Amazon was regarded as somewhat slow to jump onto the AI data-center bandwagon, the stock has been showing upward momentum since my last Seeking Alpha article on the company was published (see "Amazon: Don't Discount The Seemingly Forgotten Hyperscaler") in October. As you can see from the Seeking Alpha performance tracker in the upper right-hand corner of that article, AMZN has outperformed the S&P 500 (SP500) by 5% since that article was published last October.

One reason for the stock's recent momentum was Amazon's announcement of a $38 billion strategic partnership with OpenAI (OPENAI). It is a long-term multi-year partnership, that started immediately, and will enable OpenAI to run its advanced AI workloads on AWS’s world-class infrastructure. As a result, AWS will serve as the backbone for the ChatGPT parent's AI ambitions. The market loved it and AMZN's stock jumped 4% on the Monday following the announcement.

OpenAI CEO Sam Altman commented on the AWS partnership:

Scaling frontier AI requires massive, reliable compute. Our partnership with AWS strengthens the broad compute ecosystem that will power this next era and bring advanced AI to everyone.

"Massive" was the certainly the correct adjective: under the $38 billion agreement, which will is set to grow over the next seven years, OpenAI will be accessing hundreds of thousands of state-of-the-art NVIDIA GPUs via AWS compute, with the ability to rapidly scale agentic workloads by potentially expanding to tens of millions of CPUs. AWS already has experience at running large-scale AI infrastructure securely and reliably with clusters exceeding 500,000 compute engines.

If you read the article referenced above about OpenAI still chasing Google, it is clear the company had to do something dramatic to catch-up with GOOG's existing lead in AI infrastructure capacity. This partnership with Amazon is OpenAI's attempt to close that gap. However, note that the slide below from Amazon's Q3 presentation shows that AWS's growth rate had already accelerated significantly in Q3 (quarter ended Sept. 30th) even prior to the $38 billion deal with OpenAI was announced in November:

AWS's 20% YOY growth in Q3 compares to 17% YOY growth in Q2 and signal a re-acceleration of growth in the segment. It was the fastest growth in AWS in close to 3 years (11 quarters) while margins expanded sequentially. In addition, note the bottom line of the slide: AWS is on an annual operating income run-rate that easily exceeds $40 billion.

While AWS is the biggest and most consistent contributor to Amazon's profit profile, the company operates a powerful self-reinforcing "economic flywheel" of from a diverse set of businesses: AWS & AI Infrastructure, E-commerce, third-party sellers on Amazon.com, high-margin advertising revenue, and subscription services.

With the above as the general investment thesis, let's see what additional facts we learned from Amazon's most recent earnings report.

Q3 Earnings

Amazon's Q3 earnings report was released on October 31st, and I already mentioned AWS above. First, the big picture view:

As you can see from the segment sales, Amazon is growing all three segments (North America, International, and AWS) at double-digit rates, with its most profitable segment (AWS) growing the fastest of the three.

Peeling the onion a bit, other highlights included in the Q3 report include:

  • High-margin advertising revenue of $17.7 billion (+24% YOY).
  • Online revenue of $67.4 billion (+10% YOY).
  • Subscription services revenue of $12.6 billion (+11% YOY).
  • International operating income of $5 billion was +144% YOY.
  • Net-income of $21.2 billion ($1.95/share) compared to $15.3 billion ($1.43/share) in the year earlier quarter.

So from a high-level point of view, Amazon is firing on all cylinders.

Valuation

But, you might say, with a TTM P/E=34.5x and a forward P/E=34.2x, isn't Amazon already trading at a relatively high valuation level with all the good news baked in?

I don't think so ... because there are other catalysts working in Amazon's favor. Here are a few:

Backlog

On the Q3 conference call, CEO Andrew Jassy commented on AWS prospects moving forward:

Backlog grew to $200 billion by Q3 quarter end and doesn't include several unannounced new deals in October, which together are more than our total deal volume for all of Q3. AWS is gaining momentum. Customers want to be running their core and AI workloads in AWS given its stronger functionality, security, and operational performance, and the scale I see in front of us gives me significant confidence in what lies ahead.

The important point here is that demand for AWS capacity is clearly and significantly out-stripping supply. This is not "AI-hype," and the comment above proves it. This is why AMZN shareholders should not get overly concerned about another ramp-up of Amazon cap-ex investments to grow AWS.

Anthropic

The Q3 report included the following statement:

Third quarter 2025 net income includes pre-tax gains of $9.5 billion included in non-operating income (expense) from our investments in Anthropic, PBC.

According to Amazon's 2024 Annual Report, in Q3 of 2023 Amazon invested in a $1.25 billion note from Anthropic, PBC (ANTHRO), which is convertible to equity. In March of 2024, Amazon increased that investment by an additional $2.75 billion in a second convertible note. By late November of 2024, Anthropic announced that Amazon had invested a total of $8 billion into the company.

It's a rather complex issue and, honestly, I don't have all the information needed to figure out exactly what Amazon's current total stake in Anthropic is. This MarketWatch analysis from September of last year suggests Amazon's stake in Anthropic is in the range of 15-19%.

Regardless of exactly what the stake is, the $9.5 billion net-income uplift to Amazon's Q3 results speaks for itself (44.8% of Q3 total net-income). Perhaps even more important to AMZN shareholders is that Anthropic is very likely to go public this year through an IPO. And, just this week the Wall Street Journal reported that Anthropic recently raised an additional $10 billion which implied the total value of the company is now ~$350 billion (double its value just 4-months ago). At the midpoint of Amazon's estimated stake mentioned above (i.e. 17%), that would put Amazon's interest in Anthropic worth an estimated $59.5 billion. Not bad for an $8 billion investment, right?

But Amazon's interest in Anthropic doesn't end there by a long-shot. While many other developers of AI LLMs have arguably been focused on general chatbot performance, Anthropic's frontier model (Claude) appears to be more focused on AI automated code generation. That positions Claude as a very attractive tool for businesses and developers alike, and since Claude has been tightly integrated into Amazon's AWS cloud services, it is a boon for Amazon as well.

Trainium

Last October, Anthropic announced it planned to:

Expand our use of Google Cloud technologies, including up to one million TPUs, dramatically increasing our compute resources as we continue to push the boundaries of AI research and product development. The expansion is worth tens of billions of dollars and is expected to bring well over a gigawatt of capacity online in 2026.

That revelation shocked some Amazon investors that considered Anthropic more aligned with Amazon. AMZN shares were under pressure. But just a week after Anthropic's Google Cloud news release, Amazon said that Anthropic would end 2025 using over 1 million of Amazon's Trainium 2 compute-engines (i.e., like a Google TPU or Broadcom XPU) for LLM training and inference on the Project Rainer supercomputer. That soothed fears that Amazon's AWS relationship with Anthropic had somehow been usurped by Google.

On the Q3 conference call, Amazon CEO Andrew Jassy said:

And then on the Trainium demand, outside of our major customers. So first of all, as I mentioned on Trainium2, it's really doing well. It's fully subscribed on Trainium2. We have -- it's a multibillion-dollar business at this point. It grew 150% quarter-over-quarter in revenue.

Margin Expansion

Margins for full-year 2025 should come in close to 12%, demonstrating significant expansion over 10.8% in FY24. The margin expansion catalyst will be driven by:

  • e-Commerce efficiency gains.
  • Better logistics (better regional fulfillment/delivery and increased automation and robotics).
  • Faster growth in higher-margin businesses: primarily AWS and advertising.

These margin-expansion catalysts are likely to be in place for years to come due to Amazon's relatively wide moat surrounding all three of its business segments.

Also, it's important to note that in Q3, operating margin was only 9.7% as compared to 11.0% in Q3 of last year. However, that poor performance was primarily due to some one time charges - including a $2.5 billion settlement with the FTC and a $1.8 billion charge for severance packages. Otherwise, operating income would have been $21.7 billion - equating to a 12.0% margin (up one full percentage point YOY).

Q4 Earnings Estimate

Amazon's Q4 and full-year earnings results are due out on February 3rd, and the following graphic (courtesy of Yahoo.Finance) shows current analyst consensus estimates:

As you can see in the graphic, Q4 earnings are currently expected to come in at $1.94, a mild YOY increase (4.3%), which is also true of FY25 as compared to FY24 (far right-hand column). Again, you might say why is Amazon worth such a lofty valuation given that earnings are expected to close FY2025 at a very tepid growth rate. In my opinion, it is because the market is recognizing that Amazon is playing long-ball here with respect to its investments in AWS capacity expansion and that the investments will be well-worth it. Investors believe that because Amazon has demonstrated a successful long-term track-record when it comes to investing in infrastructure (whether it is in e-Commerce, Logistics, or AWS) that end up delivering strong returns for shareholders.

Risks

Risks include tariffs and the potential for a broad macroeconomic slowdown - including increasing uncertainty due to some rather unprecedented recent actions by the United States government.

In Q3, Amazon generated only $14.8 billion in trailing 12-months free-cash-flow as compared to $47.7 billion for the TTM ended September 30, 2024. That is a significant downturn driven by a "year-over-year increase of $50.9 billion in purchases of property and equipment." That is, primarily a cap-ex surge for AI data-center infrastructure build-out. That said, note that despite the huge up-tick in spending, Amazon is still strongly free-cash-flow positive and is not even close to having to borrow money in order to keep growing AWS at a very nice clip.

In addition, the cap-ex spend risk just mentioned is also mitigated by the fact that at the end of Q3, Amazon had cash and cash equivalents plus marketable securities totaled $94.2 billion. From my perspective, the bigger risk to Amazon would be if it were not heavily investing to take advantage of the growth opportunities in AWS, AI data-centers, OpenAI, and Anthropic.

Circular Financing Concerns

I am certainly aware of all the conversations and analysis about "circular financing" and the "AI-bubble" and "AI-hype." I share some of those concerns, but simply cannot adopted the entire narrative as it is so often presented.

I've already shown that the "AI-hype" narrative certainly does not apply to Amazon (i.e., its revenue and earnings growth profile, free-cash-flow generation, $94.2 billion in cash, etc.). That said, and as my previously referenced article on Broadcom pointed out, I certainly do think the "AI-bubble" narrative might apply to a company like Oracle (ORCL), which not only went free-cash-flow negative, but is also taking on debt. So, I would advise investors to not place all companies in the same "AI-hype" basket.

More specifically for Amazon is the $38 billion partnership with OpenAI and will OpenAI be able to come up with the funding? I don't have a crystal ball on that, but note that OpenAI is also expected to go public this year and the most current valuation I have seen is around $750 billion (see here and here). That is obviously more than $38 billion. But of course OpenAI has also made huge commitments to NVIDIA, Oracle, CoreWeave, and Google as well (among others). All that being the case, the OpenAI IPO, and its valuation level, is certainly something Amazon shareholders (and all tech investors for that matter ...) should keep an eye on.

Amazon has yet to have experienced its NVIDIA-like AI ahh-ha! moment yet. Broadcom has, Google has, Micron has - but Amazon's stock is only +55% over the past 5-years, and the company is due. I think 2026 will be Amazon's year to fully flex its AI muscles. However, I supposed I could always be wrong about that and AMZN might continue to be a relatively "middling" player in the space. But I like my chances ... and the 6.7% rise year to date (as of Thursday's close) indicates that I am not the only one.

Summary & Conclusion

While Amazon is not as showy and does not get near as much non-stop media hype as say OpenAI, Google, or even Broadcom, the company is at the very front of the AI revolution due to its #1 position as a hyperscaler, its investments in Anthropic and the integration of Claude into AWS, and thru its large self-reinforcing economic fly-wheel of businesses with which it can use AI to increase the overall monetization of its platform. Revenue should continue to grow at double-digit rates for the foreseeable future. Margin expansion will be led by e-Commerce as past cap-ex spending rolls off and with excellent growth in high-margin advertising. AWS growth is re-accelerating and the company's growth investments will write a new chapter in a new strong profit and free-cash-flow growth story. It will likely just take another year or so, but long-term AMZN investors have learned to be patient on the company's large cap-ex spending and they will eventually be well rewarded.

Tip-ranks currently has an average 12-month street estimate on Amazon's stock price of $295, or +20% from here. At ~30x my FY27 EPS estimate of $9.80/share, that seems very reasonable to me. In fact, due to the numerous positive catalysts discussed in this article, I believe the risk to that earnings estimate is to the upside.

AMZN is a Buy, and I recommend investors accumulate by dollar-cost-averaging shares. The market is going to bid-up the stock in anticipation of growth in profits and free-cash-flow that will come in the relatively near future due to cap-ex investments made in the 2H of last year and continuing up to this day.

data.title: Middlesex Water Company: Others Were More Attractive, But This Is Getting There
data.description:
In my previous article on Middlesex Water (MSEX), I rated the company a "BUY", bought shares, but also pointed out that there are other water companies that, technically speaking, are at least as, or more attractive than, this one. This is a view that I intended to update in this article for 2026, and it may no longer be true after a 13% decline (despite low valuations).

I now believe that the company's valuation in no way reflects the upside in its AEPS, its fundamental value, or the foundational premium that should be applied to a stable water company.

While I believe that time has enriched those premiums too far in most water companies, I believe we're now getting to a level where the upside in the companies is better than what the market conveys them as being. As for MSEX, it's now trading at a very significant discount to its long-term valuation that might even justify my "cheap" criterion being fulfilled. Like York Water (YORW), my favorite current water investment, this is a small company. Having declined more than 40-50% from its highs back in 2020, Middlesex now has a market capitalization of less than $1B, but despite this (again, like YORW), it comes with an institutional credit rating of A. It has very little debt, it has a decent yield that has been maintained and raised for 44 years, making it close to a dividend king, and it has prospects of double-digit forward growth, once it comes out of the 2025 fiscal.

The company absolutely needed a significant correction from its highs. This trajectory is a lesson in how much a company can become overvalued, and how much it needs to correct before it can once again be considered attractive. You can argue that I went in too early here, and you wouldn't necessarily be wrong. You can even, based on historical trends, argue that the company could decline yet another 10-20% before it bottoms - but given the earnings forecasts, I would view this as unlikely.

Middlesex Water Company - The upside just got even more attractive.

Water company safety is a high one, for all the reasons that I've mentioned previously - not just relating to things like the stable earnings of its regulated contracts, its stable relationships with the regulators and government entities that control much of its operations, but above all with the crucial and vital importance of its end product to both its residential as well as its commercial and industrial consumers/customers. Who, after all, would argue with the need of water?

Add to this the fact that in the US, water bills are still a downright negligible portion of the average expense of a household or a business (unless high-consumer or specialized), with less than 3% of its income on water bills, and I believe this is not necessarily one of the costs that government is likely to "combat", as opposed to something like healthcare, pension and eldercare. As a comparison, lower-income households typically can spend over 30% of their income on healthcare, with Medicare households at about 13.6%.

What I am saying with this is that, in my view, the household water bill is unlikely to be a target for heavy cost-cutting. In Sweden, we're already paying much more than 2% of our income on water. (3-4%, but still considered cheap to some countries).

I also happen to believe that investing in water or water-related businesses will be a very good idea on a forward basis, considering the general trend of more expensive water, as well as the ongoing trend of water scarcity across the world.

So I don't just invest in water-based industrial companies like Veolia (VEOEY), but also focus on water utilities wherever I can find them and where they are investable. Unfortunately for me, in Sweden, such businesses are still publicly owned.

Middlesex represents a solid investment with a solid upside based on a regulated business. Its unregulated businesses are, by comparison, negligible. Rate base growth rates have been slowing down over the past few years, but are impressive over the longer term.

Much of the company risk is centered, like with other water companies, on its investment plans. Like other companies in the same sector worldwide, MSEX has to focus on PFAS and chemical treatment. The single largest investment is in a PFAS treatment plant costing about $105M, with other investment costs being small by comparison. The next 3 years of investment will be 50% higher than the previous 3. It's these trends that act like a natural "weight" to the company's prospects from here on out. It requires solid rate base growth, as well as/constructive discussions with the regulator, DEPSC. Thankfully, this has been a good one, with the most recent case allowing for a $5.5M annual revenue increase and an ROE of 9.5% at 53.5% equity. The broader comparison of my covered companies in the water utility sector implies an 8.9% to 9.3% RoE.

In the latest general rate case for NJ, things look even better (provided it goes through at any rate approaching this level).

The 53.5%+ equity here is probably the more solid number, implying a very good foundation for future returns with a high portion of equity as opposed to debt. Companies in this sector tend to carry not-inconsiderable amounts of debt due to the infrastructure needs of their operations (as evidenced by this company's expansion plans), with debt that can go over 1.6x (D/E ratio) - but MSEX has only 0.87x, making it significantly less leveraged than the average. This means that the 9.5% RoE is, in my view, not enhanced in any way by taking significant risk, and this is why the company is the second pick in this sector, next to a company like YORW.

Recent results from November 2025 don't give me reason to doubt or to change this outlook going forward. Based on these metrics, I believe the company is undervalued for what it offers and what it presents here - an undervaluation that, after a 10%+ drop, presents as follows in the upside for MSEX.

Middlesex Water - One of the better upsides in water, now comparable with York Water.

For some time now, I've been comparing other water companies to YORW when comparing upsides. At a 5-year normalized forward P/E of 30x+, the company has a massive upside due to the inflated premium, so we need to be careful to move away from that premium.

I'd use the 20-year average that comes to about 25-26x P/E, implying a fair value of about $80/share for the 2028E period, which in turn would imply about $60-$62/share for a good, safe 15-20% annualized rate per year of upside, exclusive of dividends at 2-3%.

This was the basis for my $62/share PT that I gave in my last article, and continues to be my case here. The difference now is that the upside is even higher and better than previously.

Instead of about 15% with dividends, we're above this, at 20%+ with dividends each year, at the average for the past 20 years. This means even if you were to forecast the company at a significant discount, say at 18-20x P/E, you wouldn't be losing money - far from it. At 20.5x, it's still double digits, and even at 15x P/E, your downside is protected with a slight gain every year with how results look now.

The company is very forecastable with high accuracy - over 80% both on the 1-year and 2-year basis, with a very low negative forecast miss. The downside lies in the low dividend and the profitability (it's lower than other industrial companies), but this is part of what it means to operate in a regulated environment. You get safety, but you pay with lower profits and upside.

Whether you're willing to accept this "deal" is of course up to you - at the right price, I certainly am - and this is the right price.

I am therefore expanding my position in MSEX as of this article and in early 2026. My alternative investment is still YORW, which currently trades lower than MSEX does, but the issue is that this position is starting to look rather "full". Also, we've now reached a point where MSEX has a better overall upside to a lower P/E than YORW does, whose upside is about 19% annually to 26x P/E, instead of 20% to 25x P/E. The yields of the two companies are very similar, so there's no clear "winner" there.

You could make a case for Veolia still being a better investment - and I am, which is why my Veolia position is full before I invest in any of these regulated businesses. Veolia, a French business, has a better upside to an even lower P/E and a yield that's almost twice the size of that YORW and MSEX has.

It once again shows the importance of considering not just one market, not just one company, but the entire world of profitability and potential before choosing a clear direction.

Risks and considerations to the company are as follows.

Risks To My Thesis

Looking at the future, as mentioned, the one risk I see is that the combination of increased servicing and infrastructure costs, coupled with lower returns, would justify a fundamentally lower premium for water companies. This remains the risk going into 2026, and I believe the current forecast for the company's earnings confirms this. This risk is similar to YORW, but YORW is cheaper, which makes it, in turn, more attractive.

YORW's appeal also remains here, but I believe with the recent decline, it has moderated quite a bit to where both companies are now attractive, with market-beating upsides for each.

This is a risk I consider worth taking. If you're either, like me, fully invested in Veolia or similar companies, or want a non-international stock, then what you have here is a good, safe upside and what I view to be a SWAN - and I don't use that description lightly, especially these days, with the market in a quite overvalued state overall.

The biggest primary risk to MSEX is the impact of higher and higher infrastructure investment requirements, as we're seeing forecasted in both 2026 and 2027.

If these investments become as forecasted, or even larger, this will act as a drag on earnings and on dividend growth, and thus I maintain a rather conservative thesis and forecasts for the company.

My thesis for the SWAN is as follows.

Thesis

Middlesex is a solid dividend king among other water companies, which share the company's fundamental qualities and upside. The positives for these companies include a safe dividend, safe operations usually unaffected by external turmoil due to the necessary qualities of their operations. Their risks are typically limited to local environmental disasters/effects, and the need for retrofitting or addressing their service infrastructure.

I give the company a PT of $62/share, unchanged from my previous article. I therefore view Middlesex Water as a "BUY" and would consider opening a position in the company for the long term. I believe we have an annualized upside of at least 20% or higher here. I now view the company as superior to YORW; but not superior to all other investment alternatives on the market.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a price target of $62/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of January 2026.

data.title: LCNB Corp.: A Rare Mix Of Quality And Price
data.description:
I always enjoy analyzing companies from my home state of Ohio. And you don't get much closer than a firm like LCNB Corp. (LCNB). Headquartered out of Lebanon, Ohio, the company is located about three hours south from where I live. In all likelihood, I have driven by one of its branches at some point, though I certainly wouldn't remember it today. The company has been around long enough for that to have happened. In fact, it traces its roots all the way back to 1877. You would think that, given all that time, it would have had the opportunity to grow to be rather large. But with a market capitalization of only $232.5 million, it is one of the smaller companies that I have been able to dig into. Despite its small size, and even though it has experienced some financial volatility in recent years, I look upon the company in a favorable light. And at the end of the day, I believe that rating it a soft "Buy" is appropriate.

An Interesting Bank

Like many smaller banks, LCNB Corp. engages in a lot of traditional community-oriented banking activities. It provides commercial and industrial loans, commercial and residential real estate loans, agricultural loans, construction loans, SBA loans, and the like. It offers up certain consumer-oriented debt like home equity lines of credit and commercial and consumer loans. It even lends out capital for those wanting to buy cars, boats, or engage in house repairs. Naturally, it provides customers with safe deposit boxes, cashier's checks, access to ATMs, debit cards, wire transfer services, and so much more.

Having said that, it does have a degree of diversification unexpected of it for its size. For instance, it has its own wealth management division that provides trust administration, estate settlement, and fiduciary services for its clients. That part of the company also offers investment management activities when it comes to agency accounts, individual retirement accounts, foundations, endowments, and trusts. Its security brokerage services are offered through a partnership that it engages in. And the licensed brokers that it has offer a wide array of investment services. Honestly, I was kind of surprised at some of the activities that the company engages in, considering its size. But here we are.

From the 36 offices that the institution had at the end of 2024, it has been able to grow the overall size of its balance sheet in recent years. The company ended the 2024 fiscal year with $1.88 billion of deposits. That was up nicely from the $1.61 billion it had two years earlier. And for the most recent quarter, we did see a slight decline, with that dropping to $1.85 billion. But that's not a substantial drop in and of itself. The bottom line here is that, on the whole, we are seeing continued growth.

With its deposits, it can fund the origination of loans. The company ended the third quarter of 2025 with $1.67 billion worth on its books. That compares to the $1.40 billion it had at the end of 2022. The most significant exposure that the institution has when it comes to its loan portfolio would be the $497 million tied up in non-owner-occupied commercial real estate. But honestly, the institution is very diverse. For instance, it has $383.5 million of residential real estate loans that are secured by senior liens on one- to four-family dwellings. It boasts $233.6 million of commercial multifamily property exposure, and it has $222.6 million of owner-occupied commercial real estate exposure. Other categories exist, but they are much smaller by comparison, with examples being commercial and industrial loans, consumer loans, agricultural loans, and the like.

The value of securities has, in recent years, dropped modestly. From 2022 through the third quarter of 2025, these declined from $314.1 million to $265.5 million. Over that same period, however, cash increased, expanding from $22.7 million to $36.1 million. And even though debt is up since 2022, since 2023 it has dropped sharply, declining from $210.5 million to $104.7 million.

Normally, you would expect growth in the balance sheet to also lead to growth in the income statement. But that's not always the case. The last few years have been particularly challenging for the financial sector. Although you would think that higher interest rates would prove positive, that is not always the case. The fact of the matter is that assets tend to adjust gradually, while certain liabilities like deposits adjust immediately. So as interest rates rose, this company and others like it saw their net interest margins contract. That drop from 3.55% in 2022 to 2.91% in 2024 caused net interest income to dip from $60.8 million to $58.8 million. However, we did see a nice increase in net interest income in the first nine months of 2025, with the $51.5 million reported by the company comfortably exceeding the $42.8 million reported the previous year. Some of this change is because of a $0.9 million decline in provision for credit losses. But most of it seems to have been attributable to a surge in net interest margin from 2.81% to 3.43%.

The good news is that, even though net interest margin has been volatile, non-interest margin has been growing consistently. It went from $14.3 million in 2022 to $20.4 million in 2024. For the first nine months of 2025, it amounted to $16.2 million. That beat out the $14.4 million reported the previous year. Much of that gain can be attributed to greater fiduciary income and a rise in service charges and fees on deposit accounts. This helped to push net profits up, with a reading of $17.5 million in the first nine months of 2025 exceeding the $7.4 million reported the previous year. This follows years of volatility.

When it comes to valuation, LCNB Corp. is not great, but it's not awful either. Assuming that the rest of 2025 looked like the first nine months did on a year-over-year basis, we would get a price-to-earnings multiple for the company of exactly 10. That is at the very high end of what I typically like to see. And as the chart above illustrates, this would make the company cheaper than any of the five banks that I stacked it up against. On a price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book basis, it looks relatively attractive also. In the chart below, you can see that only one of the companies is cheaper than it is in the first case, while two of them are cheaper when it comes to the second. The fact that it is trading at a discount to book value is also encouraging.

Another thing that I appreciate about LCNB Corp. is the fact that asset quality is relatively high. I typically like to see a return on assets reading of 1% or greater. But as the first chart below illustrates, our candidate has a reading of 1.21%. That puts it tied as the highest between it and the five banks that I compared it to. In the subsequent chart, you can see a return on equity rating of 10.73%. My preference is for this to be 10% or greater. So in addition to passing that, the company came in above three of the five banks that I stacked it up against.

We should also touch briefly on credit quality metrics. And this is another area where the institution shines. During the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, only 0.12% of its loans were classified as non-performing. That's well below the 0.50% maximum that I typically like to see. And it's down from the 0.27% reading that we get using data at the end of 2024. Over that same window of time, we have seen non-performing assets drop from 0.20% to 0.09%. That also happens to be well below the maximum that I look for. So that's great.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, it is my opinion that the market is not adequately valuing LCNB Corp. The last few years have been tough, but the change in interest rates should be bullish for the company. Asset quality is high, and credit quality metrics are good. The stock is cheap in some respects, though not quite as cheap as I would love for it to be. But on the whole, this paints a favorable picture, justifying a soft "Buy" rating at this time.

data.title: Opendoor: The 'Best-Case' Scenario Leads To Disappointment
data.description:
Opendoor Technologies Inc. (OPEN) is rallying yet again, this time not solely due to a meme frenzy. The new incoming management team has given ambitious turnaround plans with targets for GAAP breakeven by the end of this year. President Trump appears to have plans to lower mortgage rates, which might simultaneously reduce OPEN’s financing costs as well as accelerate home transactions. These both point to strong potential momentum for the financials, perhaps explaining the enthusiasm for the stock. The valuation, however, looks stretched even under unrealistic assumptions. I am downgrading the stock to a Strong Sell rating.

OPEN Stock Price

I last covered OPEN in August, where I downgraded the stock to a Sell rating amidst a then-meme rally. The stock has soared around 71% since.

Some of that rally has come in recent trading as President Trump aims to reduce mortgage rates through $200 billion of institutional mortgage bond purchases. Between the potential for lower mortgage rates and management’s plan to turn the ship around, there’s a lot to like here. Valuation, however, is a nagging worry.

OPEN Stock Key Metrics

OPEN has historically been an “i-buyer,” meaning that it offers liquidity to the market by purchasing homes at a discount to market with the aim to quickly flip the homes at market prices. In the most recent quarter, OPEN saw contribution margins decline 160 bps YoY, leading adjusted EBITDA margins to decline by 80 bps.

On the conference call, management blamed the lower contribution margins as being due to moving through older inventory. Management outlined their transformation plan as centering around becoming “kind of like a market maker in the future,” with the idea “to profit from flow, speed, and tight spreads, not on bets on the direction of the economy.” I actually like that direction. When I had previously purchased a stake in the company during the pandemic (losing considerable sums in the process), my hope was for the company to re-position as an asset-light marketplace for home buyers and sellers. Perhaps the company might be finally making a concerted move towards that vision. Management set a target for “adjusted net income profitability by the end of 2026, measured on a forward 12-month basis.” Management expects to reach this by increasing their scale to “buying somewhere around 6,000 homes” by exiting the fourth quarter of 2026, with a “contribution margin of 5% to 7% as we approach the acquisitions with renewed rigor, decreasing tail homes, improving days in possession, and therefore, holding costs.” Management also expects to reduce financing costs to “2% to 3% of revenue” with “adjusted OpEx of 3% to 4% of revenue.” Together with the potential for lower mortgage rates, it looks like the stars might finally be aligning for the company.

Is OPEN Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As a valuation-focused investor, I caution that an enticing narrative is not typically enough to make for a reliable bullish thesis. At recent prices, OPEN was trading at around a $7.5 billion market cap.

It can be exciting to think about how much the business might improve if management can execute on their turnaround plans and if mortgage rates can fall. But we must put a number in that instead of just directly purchasing the stock. I like to do this by assuming a better-than-best-case scenario and looking at valuation then. We can see below that OPEN’s business peaked in the 2022 year when they generated $15.6 billion in revenue and $667 million in gross profits.

In 2022, the company purchased 34,962 homes and sold 39,183 homes. In 2021, the company had purchased 36,908 homes and sold 21,725 homes. The company generated a 1.1% adjusted EBITDA loss in 2022 and a 0.7% adjusted EBITDA profit in 2021. Mortgage rates were at very low levels at just under 3% in 2021.

Now we can estimate the better-than-best-case scenario. Management has guided to purchasing around 6,000 homes in the fourth quarter, exiting 2026. Let’s interpret that to mean 24,000 in home purchases on an annual basis. Let’s instead assume that the company instead reaches the 2021/2022 peak levels of purchasing around 35,000 homes and selling 39,000 homes. Let’s assume that the company achieves the high end of the target contribution margin of 7%. Let’s assume zero other financing costs and adjusted operating expenses at 3% of revenues. Let’s then assume the company generates 4% GAAP net margins (compare this to the 0.7% adjusted EBITDA margin in 2021). If we assume a $15.6 billion revenue run rate, then that would equate to around $622 million in GAAP net income. The stock is currently trading at around 12x that number.

Given that these financial results would likely represent the high point of a cyclical market, I view that valuation to be too high, especially when compared to a home builder like Toll Brothers (TOL), which trades at around 10.5x 2026e earnings. For reference, TOL's 5-year average valuation stands at around 8x earnings.

OPEN Stock Conclusion

OPEN management appears to have a solid plan in place to turn things around. A reduction in mortgage rates certainly would not hurt such efforts. I, however, cannot ignore the lofty valuation at which the stock trades at, which looks expensive even if one assumes a quick return to peak pandemic levels. I am downgrading the stock to a Strong Sell rating.

data.title: Barrick Mining: Why This Could Be A Bad Time To Buy Gold Miners
data.description:
My title may sound strange to some at first. How can Barrick Mining Corporation (B) be at a bad time to buy? Well, even though the momentum seems as good as it gets, I personally believe that the bearish scenario has been largely overlooked lately and almost dismissed in favor of the company's quality and the bullish trends of gold (XAUUSD:CUR) (GLD).

And indeed, at a time of global geopolitical uncertainty, rising tensions, economic fears, and everything else, having exposure to gold in your portfolio may seem interesting, especially through a company like Barrick Mining, which still has several other catalysts to unlock upside for the stock next year. Still, I believe that the pessimistic scenario, coupled with the valuation and the dangerous consensus positioning around B stock, is why my rating is a Sell.

Barrick's Upside Risks

Before going through my bear case, I need to say that Barrick's case is really interesting, and there is no shortage of upside risks. We are talking about one of the world's leading gold companies, which still has copper production that cannot be ignored and has recently been helping to diversify revenue. In addition, its financials have been extremely positive in recent quarters, not only due to the price of gold but also internal factors such as the company's margin.

For example, in Q3, revenue jumped 23% YoY, while EBITDA grew 57%, reaching $2.02 billion. And the company is demonstrating some strong discipline in capital allocation, expecting to sell non-core assets, pocketing another $2.6 billion in fiscal year 2025. By Q3, it had already made more than $1 billion in buybacks, distributed $600 million in dividends, and invested more than $900 million in growth.

In other words, we are talking about a company that is growing, optimizing its portfolio, is a cash cow, and is directly exposed to one of the best commodities in the world.

But in addition to this good structure, there are several other cyclical triggers. One of the most interesting, in my view, was the resolution of the operation in Mali, which greatly reduces geographical uncertainties in that region and is a positive prospect for the resumption of production. As mentioned by Eliana Scialabba (check the article here), this removes a risk that was well perceived by the market. In addition, we also have portfolio optimization beyond divestments, such as the IPO of gold assets in North America, which should unlock shareholder value, and with the current valuation and momentum of gold, it seems to me an excellent time to do an IPO.

The Gold “Problem”

Investing in gold seems fascinating. It is a very useful metal for the composition of various electronics, is used in jewelry, and also has the effect of hedging against inflation. But lately, most of what I just mentioned has mattered little. The price of gold has risen sharply, with the GLD ETF gaining more than 130% over the past five years.

But looking at historical performance, we already have some hints as to how this was an outlier. 2025 was the best year for gold in a long time, and note how its performance differs from other bullish periods such as 2020, when the outlook for gold was also positive for several reasons, and yet the asset rose 24.6%, which is great, but seems small compared to the +66% of 2025.

The reason for this was not increased demand from factories or jewelers, but rather from central banks. For various reasons, such as lower confidence in the USD, greater geopolitical instability, and increased tensions, central banks around the world have significantly increased their purchases of gold. With the recent capture of Maduro and tensions in Israel, the main hint is that the geopolitical scenario will not cool down anytime soon; in fact, it may become even more unstable, which would be another reason to be bullish on gold.

But the problem, for me, is precisely that. There is a very large consensus on gold. I don't like to be contrarian just for the sake of being contrarian, but it seems extremely difficult to me that we will see such strong performance continue for long periods. It would require this combination of factors, mainly the willingness of central banks to continue buying tons and tons of gold, to maintain this bullish sentiment.

On the other hand, it is easy to see a drawdown scenario. Consider if, throughout 2026, we have resolutions to some conflicts, the US economy has no negative surprises, and central banks normalize their gold purchases, or perhaps even sell a little. A slight pessimism about gold could also cause speculative investors to sell, see a weaker technical chart, and so on. Note in the gold price table that this is not exactly uncommon; we usually have good years followed by more normal years or some price correction.

In addition, look at the chart below and see how, even though it is a more stable commodity than oil, the gold price still reaches historic highs several times, and after that, it is traded at discounts of -10%, -20%, -30%, and even -50% for long periods. In other words, it is also a commodity that is susceptible to substantial price increases due to euphoria or something analogous to a bubble and then corrects.

What I mean here is not that I am bearish on gold for 2026 and think that the commodity should fall 30% or 40%. In fact, conceptually, it appears to me to still be a bullish commodity, but the problem is the price and the widespread euphoria, disregarding the fact that a bearish scenario could indeed happen. Furthermore, even if 2026 is a stable year for gold, or even if the price grows again in double digits, what about 2027? And 2028? At some point, normalization must occur, in my view. I do not believe in a scenario where central banks continue to sustain this for several years in a row, along with institutional investors, individuals, etc.

Barrick’s Valuation

The problem with the price of gold would not exactly be a big problem for Barrick, nor would it be sufficient reason for a sell rating. But it turns out that Barrick’s valuation currently depends on a continuation of this bullish scenario.

The forward P/E is 20x, which may seem low, but over the last five years, the average has been 16.9x, and as I mentioned, the last five years have been bullish for gold, so we are dealing with a timeframe where the multiple already priced in some revenue gains.

Going deeper into the valuation, we see that market expectations for the next two years are still very strong. In 2026, the market expects Barrick's revenue to grow 26%, even with some divestitures. As for EPS, we are talking about an estimated growth of 48% in 2026, followed by 11% in 2027.

Mixing these ups and downs in commodity prices, Barrick delivered a CAGR of 4.6% for revenue over 10 years, a much more modest assumption than the market expects for the coming years.

In other words, even if the valuation is not extremely stretched, it allows for a drawdown in the event of a negative surprise in the price of gold, and also ends up compressing shareholder yield.

The Bottom Line

I don't think Barrick is a company to short; in fact, as I mentioned in the title, it is a good company. But the “historic peak” of gold and a consensus that the commodity will continue to rise and everything will work out end up reducing Barrick's margin of safety, since its valuation is already pricing in these positive catalysts. Therefore, my rating is a Sell, signaling that I prefer to avoid B stock for now and eventually return when there is a more predictable window and a better margin of safety.

data.title: Coherent: Very High Return Since July But Here Is Why I Pass Today
data.description:
Article Thesis

I covered Coherent Corp. (COHR) in July, and rated it as a “Buy” with the stock slightly above $99, due to a combination of undervaluation and future growth, this being what I'm looking at for a GARP (Growth At A Reasonable Price) strategy.

With most of its business in optoelectronic components and devices used in data centers, Coherent was (and is) poised to benefit from increased needs of network speed and bandwidth, or of lower consumption and latency.

Finally, a last contributing factor for my decision was their operating leverage, especially due to a decreasing SG&A margin coming with higher scale, which is exactly what I want to see for these growth-oriented companies.

I implemented a short Put Spread position through options, with some extra downside protection, and that was highly profitable; however, the stock surged even higher. I will detail below some comparisons between the two, but also why I am on the sidelines today, after a Fair Value reassessment.

Business Highlights

First, I want to look at the fiscal year 2025, which ended in June. If we look at end markets, their Datacenter & Communications market continued to grow at an impressive pace, with ~60% of total revenue, while Industrial and Other continued to struggle:

Or, if we look from another angle, business segments, their Networking segment continued its impressive march, reaching ~62% of the business in Q4’25 from ~58% in Q3. At the same time, Lasers and Materials continued to struggle:

Then, among some notable performances from the last two quarters, they reported:

  • First revenue from their OCS (Optical Circuit Switch), an important new technology for scale-out data-center architectures.
  • Industry-first 600W Excimer Laser for emerging energy applications, encouraging news from one of their struggling business segments, Lasers.
  • A little later, in September, next-generation instruments for testing optical networks.
  • Strong demand for the newly launched 1.6T optical transceivers, highlighted during their last earnings call, on top of continuous growth for the 800G ones.

I find the last point especially important, since optical transceivers are by far Coherent’s most important product in their most important market (Datacenter and Communications) and in their most important business segment (Networking). Taking a look at their market share, it seems that Coherent is in pole position with 20%-25% estimated market share, or at least among the 5 major players.

However, this market share is not a given in the future. Although this is a high growing market, the market is relatively fragmented, with other names besides the above ones. For example, I wrote two bullish articles about POET Technologies (POET), a new producer of optical engines, the main component of an optical transceiver.

Financial Highlights

Let’s take a look at what happened in the last two quarters since my previous article. The outlook that they provided in August for fiscal Q1’26 was a bit disappointing:

The stock initially fell by ~20% after the earnings report, with investors likely expecting a beat and raise like for the peer Lumentum (LITE), but disappointed with the results and guidance. However, analysts remained mostly bullish, continuing to see “strong secular tailwinds benefiting the broad industry”.

The actual results from November came in better than expected:

Both revenue and gross margin came at the higher end of their outlook, while EPS even exceeded it. And this time, the stock jumped immediately after earnings, with investors more satisfied about the results and outlook for the next quarter:

Long term, they kept the same 42% non-GAAP gross margin target, which corresponds to ~39% GAAP gross margin.

Non-GAAP operating margin target is at least 24%, corresponding to 17.5%–18.5% GAAP operating margin. In my article from July, I modeled a 15% margin in 2030 and 19% in 2034, but I will now increase these projections to 18.5% in 2030 (in line with their model) because they already grew their non-GAAP operating margin to 19.5% in the last quarter, compared to 18% in the prior quarter and 16.1% in the year ago quarter, clearly demonstrating their operating leverage. For 2034, I will model operating margin only slightly increasing to 21%, with slower revenue growth and less operating leverage after 2030.

Finally, after paying down $400 million in debt, Coherent’s Debt/EBITDA decreased since my last article from ~3.6x to slightly less than 3x today. I estimated that “they should be comfortably under that threshold one year from now, at the end of FY’26”, and it seems that they already are. And, with this powerful organic growth, it seems that this trajectory is sustainable, an important condition required by Fitch to upgrade their BB credit rating.

Fair Value

Looking at the consensus revenue estimates, we observe some big upward revisions after their November earnings:

While I usually agree with consensus for the current year because there is more visibility, for Coherent I was already more optimistic in July for the following 2 years, modeling $7.12B revenue for fiscal ’27 compared to $6.99B consensus, and $7.90B for fiscal ’28, compared to $7.2B consensus. I didn’t understand such a pessimistic view for a company well positioned in such a growing industry.

And indeed, after these revisions, and after Coherent’s improved outlook, I am much closer to consensus today, modeling $7.61B in fiscal ’27 (14% YoY) and $8.51B in fiscal ’28 (12% YoY).

My other assumptions for a DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) model are:

  • Revenue growth (after fiscal ’28): gradually decreasing from 11% to 5% in 2035.
  • EBIT margin: 18.5% in fiscal 2030 and 21% in 2035 (see above section).
  • Tax rate: 19% (the company is estimating 18%-20% for the next quarter).
  • Growth in perpetuity: 3%, in line with GDP growth.
  • WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital): 10.05%, coming from 10.5% cost of equity (90% weight in the capital structure) and 6% cost of debt. Compared to my previous article, I decreased the cost of equity from 11.5% to 10.5%, due to slightly lower expected risk-free rate, lower indebtedness, and a slightly stronger competitive position. However, WACC witnessed only a minor correction from 10.12% to 10.05% because the weight of equity in the capital structure increased from ~75% to ~90%.

Resulting Fair Value is $154.8, about 19% potential downside from the current price ($191.62), and up from $115.15 in July due to several reasons: partly due to some passage of time with naturally higher cash-flows, partly due to improved revenue/EBIT outlook, partly due to a slightly lower WACC.

For a sensitivity analysis, I do several types of exercises. One of them is a reverse DCF: what input parameters are needed to justify today’s price? And I try to vary the parameters that I consider with the highest uncertainty. In this case, and generally for GARP stocks, the highest uncertainty is for future revenue growth. Consequently, EBIT margin will vary a bit, due to increased or decreased scale.

Therefore, in my reverse DCF model, I changed the following parameters:

  • Revenue growth: 14% from 2027 to 2030, and 9% from 2031 to 2035.
  • EBIT margin: 20% in 2030 and 22% in 2035.

With these modifications, Fair Value is $194, which was yesterday’s price. This is not such a big difference for a growth industry: in the first model, we have $14B estimated 2035 revenue, in the second model, $17.33B.

We could say that we should also model a slightly pessimistic scenario, and I could agree with that. However, that scenario would be offset by the growth. Suppose Fair Value would be 20% lower than my base-case $154.8, but with 14% revenue growth and clearly more profit growth, Fair Value could easily be back at this level in one year or even less.

Therefore, the only rating that I have in mind for now is “Hold”.

Very High Return From A Short Put Spread And Even Higher From Stock

Back in July, I initiated the following short Put Spread strategy:

  • Short COHR 85 Put Jan’26 for $8.9/share.
  • Long COHR 80 Put Jan’26 for $7/share.

Per total, I had a credit of $1.9/share (or $190 per contract), for a cost of $5 (spread between strike prices) - $1.9 (collected premium) = $3.1/share.

This position will expire worthless next week for the maximum profit of $1.9/share, or 1.9/3.1 = 61.2% ROI in 6 months. Or I could close the position today for something very close because both put options are so far OTM (Out-Of-The-Money).

However, the stock performed even better, bringing close to 105% in these 6 months. Let’s see the reasons why I had a short Put Spread, and not a stock position:

  • The short Put Spread is much more versatile, it could have brought this 61% return even with the stock flat or slightly down (COHR above $85, compared to ~$93.3 when I Initiated the position). For example, it brought more than 100% in a year for iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT), with TLT at about the same level. Now, let’s say that this is a weak reason because with COHR at $93.3 and with an increasing role of optics in data centers, there was a very low probability for the stock to go down.
  • It was very difficult to predict the stock gaining more than 61.2% in less than 6 months. From $93.3, that would have meant ~$151, and my Fair Value estimate was $115.15 back then. Of course, I did expect a little more because Fair Value naturally grows in time once with the cash-flows, but for the other part (improved outlook) I usually prefer to be a bit on the conservative side. This is a strong reason, 61% in 6 months with very high probability is much better than more than 61% with very low probability.
  • In my stock portfolio, I already had (and still have) a large exposure to data centers, and I wanted more diversification. This is a moderate reason.

Risks

I specified more fundamental risks in my article from July, and I don’t want to insist on more today. I no longer have any position.

But I talked in the previous section about the beauty of a short Put Spread strategy, it gains in three directions: with the stock up, flat or even slightly down. Then why not such a strategy again today? Well, a high reward / zero-risk strategy has not been presented yet, and yes, this strategy gains even with the stock “slightly” down. But with the stock just a bit more down, those high profits can quickly turn to high losses. For example, let’s take a hypothetical example:

With a cost of $550 and maximum profit of $450 (although probably a little lower because these are average bid-ask prices), we can observe a ROI of up to ~81% (450/$550) with COHR above $165 in July, but -100% with COHR below $155.

Sure, by managing the position early we could limit this loss to somewhere between 50% and 80% (because those options will still have extrinsic value), but the question is: how confident am I that COHR will be above $165? With a base-case Fair Value estimate of ~$154, not that confident to risk such a big loss. For a reward/risk = ~1 (~80%/~80%), I want a much higher probability of success. That’s why I pass today.

Takeaway

Coherent's growth is fueled by data center demand of optoelectronics, with a leadership position in the optical transceivers market. Operating leverage is driving margin expansion, with decreasing SG&A margin.

Recent results exceeded guidance, next quarter outlook was well received by the market, and Debt/EBITDA fell below 3x, supporting a sustainable growth trajectory and improved credit outlook.

My updated DCF yields a Fair Value of $154.8, up from $115.15 in July, due partly to some passage of time with naturally higher cash-flows, partly to improved revenue/EBIT outlook, and partly to a slightly lower WACC. With ~19% potential downside from the current price, but with double-digit growth prospects, a 'Hold' rating is now warranted after the stock’s strong run.

A short Put Spread strategy offered 61.2% return in 6 months, with the stock advancing close to 105%, but with a much higher probability for a short Put Spread, since it can have that return with the stock up, flat, or even slightly down. But with base-case Fair Value near $154, a short Put Spread strategy now offers an unattractive risk/reward.
